content.full_text:
January 23, 1992

William E. Szczepaniak  
4085 Long Beach Boulevard  
Long Beach, CA 90807

Re: Your Request for Advice  
Our File No. I-91-542

Dear Mr. Szczepaniak:

You have requested assistance under the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Because your request is a general inquiry, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).2/

In your letter and subsequent telephone conversations, including our most recent conversation of January 8, 1992 when I provided general advice, you asked for clarification of the reporting obligations, if any, for a political organization.

As described in your letter and our telephone conversations, the organization you represent is a local group of persons who promote the interests of the Republican Party. The group is not an official party organization, and it does not make monetary or non-monetary contributions to support or oppose candidates or ballot measures. The total amount of funds raised by the organization is in the range of $1,500 annually, and these funds are used for such expenses as a headquarters and other costs associated with the organization's activities. The primary activities of the organization include distributing Republican Party literature, answering questions from the public, voter registration, and assisting candidates with typical campaign activities such as processing campaign materials (e.g., stuffing envelopes) paid for by the candidates' campaign committees.

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

An organization that receives $1,000 or more in a calendar year for "political purposes" qualifies as a committee under the provisions of the Act and incurs reporting obligations. (Sections 82013(a) and 82015.) The primary guidelines for determining what constitute "political purposes" are contained in Regulations 18215 and 18225 which are enclosed.

Based on the facts provided, the organization you represent does not appear to qualify as a committee and, therefore, will not have a reporting obligation because most of the funds raised by the organization are not for political purposes. In addition, it appears that many of the activities of members of the organization are "volunteer personal services" that are exempt from the Act's definition of "contribution." (Section 82015.)

As we discussed in our telephone conversations, funds spent by the organization for partisan voter registration and get-out-the-vote activities are expenditures for political purposes. However, you stated that the proportion of the organization's annual expenditures for such political purposes is less than $1,000. Based on these facts, the organization would not qualify as a committee under the Act.

I hope this letter has been of assistance. Should you have any questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jay Greenwood
Political Reform Consultant
